Travel

Artificial Intelligence

Software Development

Web Development

Product Management

12 min read
Just now

--

How TrailVerse evolved through broken layouts, generic AI answers, changing product questions, and a deeper understanding of how travelers actually plan trips
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A little over a year ago, TrailVerse was a simple national park explorer running on a temporary Vercel URL.
The homepage had a large map covered with blue markers, a search box, a few filters, and buttons for park events and favorites. It worked, but it was still much closer to a directory than a complete travel product.
Recently, I went back through screenshots of those early versions.
Some were exciting to see again. Others were painful.
There were overflowing mobile cards, placeholder images, incorrect mileage calculations, repeated error messages, inconsistent navigation, and more gradients than any travel platform probably needed.
But beneath the rough design and unfinished features, I could see the beginning of almost every important idea that exists in TrailVerse today.
That was the surprising part.
The current product was not designed in one moment and then gradually implemented. It was discovered through building, testing, failing, and repeatedly changing the questions I was trying to answer.

The first version answered a simple question
The earliest TrailVerse was essentially a national park directory.
Users could explore a map of the United States, search for parks, apply basic filters, view park events, and create an account to save favorites.
The original question was straightforward:
Where are the national parks, and how can their information be easier to explore?
That first version was limited, but the underlying idea was useful. Information about national parks existed across many pages and systems, and I wanted to make exploration simpler.
Even then, I was already thinking beyond a static directory. Events, favorites, accounts, maps, and trip planning appeared early in the product.
But the experience mainly helped people locate parks and browse basic details.
It did not yet help them make a travel decision.
Finding Yellowstone on a map is useful. Deciding whether Yellowstone is the right destination for your dates, budget, group, interests, and travel style is a much harder problem.
At that point, TrailVerse had not yet learned the difference.

Adding more features did not automatically create a better product
The next phase grew quickly.
I added seasonal park recommendations, different browsing views, trip dashboards, estimated budgets, mileage totals, saved collections, event categories, user profiles, travel statistics, and mobile navigation.
From a feature-count perspective, TrailVerse looked much more substantial.
But it also became less coherent.
The product was trying to prove that it could do many things:

• Display parks on a map

• Recommend destinations by season

• Show events

• Save favorites

• Create trips

• Estimate budgets and distance

• Display user travel statistics

• Offer basic planning tools
Individually, many of those ideas made sense. Together, they did not yet form a clear journey for the traveler.
The screens reflected that confusion.
Every feature wanted attention. Colors competed with one another. Cards became too large. Some data was incorrect. Errors interrupted the experience. The interface was expanding faster than the product’s underlying structure.
That phase taught me one of the most important lessons from the entire project:
A collection of related features is not automatically a coherent product.
TrailVerse could show people many things about national parks, but it still did not answer the question travelers actually cared about:
Given my dates, interests, budget, group, and travel style, where should I go and what should I do?
That question changed the direction of the product.

The first AI planner was the real turning point
The first AI trip planner guided the traveler through four steps.
First, they selected a national park.
Then they entered their travel dates and group size.
Next, they chose interests such as hiking, photography, wildlife, camping, scenic drives, water activities, history, or stargazing.
Finally, they selected a budget level, fitness level, and accommodation preference.
This flow represented a major change in how I was thinking about TrailVerse.
Most people do not know how to write a complete travel-planning prompt.
They may ask an AI system to “plan a Yellowstone trip” without mentioning that they are traveling with children, prefer scenic drives, cannot complete strenuous hikes, want dark-sky photography, or only have two full days.
The guided planner turned those hidden considerations into visible product decisions.
Instead of expecting travelers to know what information the AI needed, the product collected that context step by step.
The early implementation was far from perfect.
The AI could produce long, overly enthusiastic responses. Sometimes it failed to reach the provider entirely. It could generate an itinerary, but it did not yet have strong access to changing information such as road closures, permits, current weather, operating hours, or live park programs.
Still, the core product insight was correct:
TrailVerse should not only display park information. It should understand what kind of trip the traveler wants.
That early planner became the foundation for Trailie. Today, travelers can still use a guided “Plan My Trip” flow, or start with a free-form conversation when they already know what they want to ask.

Personalization created a harder problem: trust
Once TrailVerse could generate itineraries, the next limitation became obvious.
A travel answer can sound detailed and confident while still being impractical or outdated.
Real trips depend on information that changes:

• Weather

• Park alerts

• Road and trail closures

• Seasonal access

• Permits and reservations

• Parking

• Operating hours

• Ranger programs

• Fees

• Crowd patterns
A generic language model may create a convincing itinerary without knowing that a road is closed, a permit is required, a visitor center is operating seasonally, or a popular area becomes difficult to access during certain months.
That changed the technical direction of TrailVerse.
The goal was no longer simply:
Collect preferences and send them to an AI model.
It became:
Understand the traveler, retrieve useful park information, account for current conditions when they are available, and turn those facts into a more realistic plan.
That shift led to structured tools, richer park data, live information, better search, and a more deliberate AI-planning system.
Trailie could no longer be treated as a chatbot placed beside the product.
It needed to become the intelligence layer connecting the rest of the product.

TrailVerse began answering four different travel questions
Looking back, the evolution of TrailVerse can be understood through four questions.
1. Where can I go?
The original map and park directory helped people locate destinations.
That was the starting point, but it assumed the traveler already knew what they wanted.
Many people do not begin with a park name. They begin with an idea.
They may want:

• A quiet weekend

• A family-friendly park

• Good stargazing

• Wildlife viewing

• Beginner-friendly hiking

• A historic site

• A destination near their state

• A scenic road trip
The product needed to understand those starting points too.
2. Which destination fits me?
The next generation introduced activity-based discovery, park types, topics, states, seasonal intent pages, search across parks, and side-by-side comparisons.
A traveler can now begin with the kind of experience they want rather than the exact destination.
The comparison experience also helps answer common questions such as:

• Zion or Bryce Canyon?

• Yellowstone or Grand Teton?

• Yosemite or Sequoia?

• Arches or Canyonlands?

• Glacier or Rocky Mountain?
Instead of merely showing destinations, TrailVerse began helping travelers choose between them.
That is a very different product problem.
A directory organizes information.
A planning platform helps reduce uncertainty.
3. What do I need to know before deciding?
The current park pages are much deeper than the original cards.
A traveler can research operating hours, entrance fees, current alerts, permits, weather forecasts, things to see, activities, self-guided tours, visitor centers, accommodations, parking, amenities, brochures, photos, videos, webcams, reviews, crowd calendars for many major parks, nearby lodging, food, fuel, attractions, directions, planning guides, and practical questions.
The goal is not to display more information simply because it is available.
The goal is to answer the questions that may prevent someone from confidently planning a trip.

• Can I enter without a reservation?

• How crowded will it be?

• Where should I stay?

• What weather should I expect?

• Are there active alerts?

• What requires a permit?

• How much time do I need?

• Where can I find food or fuel nearby?

• What should I prioritize if I only have two days?
The park page became less like a destination profile and more like a planning hub.
4. How do I turn that decision into a real trip?
Trailie connects the research to action.
A traveler can name a destination or describe the kind of trip they want. Trailie can help with destination selection, comparison, dates, pace, group needs, interests, budget, and itinerary structure — grounded in live park data when TrailVerse has it.
The resulting plan is no longer intended to disappear inside a one-time AI response.
It can become something the traveler refines, saves, shares, exports as a PDF, and returns to later.
That creates a more complete planning journey:
Discover → compare → verify → plan → use during the trip
That connected journey is the biggest difference between the earliest TrailVerse and the current product.

The current TrailVerse is not simply the old version with better design
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The visual improvement is significant.
The older versions relied heavily on bright gradients, emojis, oversized cards, and inconsistent component styles.
The current design is more restrained. It uses stronger outdoor photography, clearer typography, more whitespace, and a consistent green, white, and charcoal visual system.
But the deeper improvement is conceptual.
The old TrailVerse presented a collection of features:
Here is a map.
Here are some parks.
Here are events.
Here is an AI planner.
The current TrailVerse is organized around a traveler’s decision:
Where should I go, what do I need to know, and how can I turn that information into a practical trip?
That is the real transformation.
The current homepage does not begin by announcing that parks exist in a database.
It begins with an outcome:
Plan smarter national park trips.
Everything underneath supports that promise.
Popular destinations provide an easy entry point. Park of the Day offers daily inspiration. From there, travelers can move into the map for geographic context, compare parks side by side, open detailed park pages, browse planning guides, check live events, and plan with Trailie.
Earlier, these features existed beside one another.
Now they increasingly support one another.
That is the point where a project begins becoming a platform.

The map also tells the story of the product
The earliest map was covered with oversized blue markers and basic controls.
Its main purpose was to answer:
Where are the parks?
The current map includes National Parks, other National Park Service sites, things to see, campgrounds, and integrated search.
It begins answering a much richer question:
What exists around this destination, and how could it fit into my trip?
That change may look visual, but it reflects a larger shift.
The map is no longer the identity of TrailV
